meta.name: Papa a la Huancaína
meta.subtitle: Boiled potatoes with creamy ají amarillo cheese sauce

source.name: SSA Canonical Catalogs
notes: Classic Peruvian starter/salad: potatoes topped with huancaína sauce, typically served with lettuce, hard-boiled egg, and olives.
This version includes deterministic substitutions for ají amarillo, queso fresco, and evaporated milk.

summary: A classic Peruvian dish of tender potatoes served cool or room temperature, topped with a silky, slightly spicy ají amarillo cheese sauce, often garnished with egg and olives.

yield.notes: Serves 6 as a starter/side; 4 as a light meal.

heat.note: Adjust by the amount/strength of ají amarillo.

optional.label: Immersion blender
optional.notes: Works well if you make the sauce in a tall container.

notes: Gluten-free if you use gluten-free crackers/bread for the sauce.

ingredients.label: Yellow potatoes (Yukon gold or similar), scrubbed
ingredients.notes: Peruvian yellow potatoes are ideal; Yukon gold is the best common substitute.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt
ingredients.notes: Includes salt for boiling water and sauce; adjust to taste depending on cheese saltiness.

ingredients.label: Ají amarillo paste (or ají amarillo from jar)
ingredients.notes: Start with 2 tbsp if sensitive to heat or if paste is strong.

ingredients.label: Red onion, roughly chopped
ingredients.notes: Optional in some home versions; adds bite. If using, blend thoroughly.

ingredients.label: Garlic
ingredients.notes: Optional; keep minimal so it doesn't dominate.

ingredients.label: Queso fresco (or mild farmer cheese), crumbled
ingredients.notes: Substitute: feta (rinse + squeeze dry) or ricotta salata for a tangier profile.

ingredients.label: Evaporated milk
ingredients.notes: Substitute: whole milk + 2 tbsp cream; or half-and-half.

ingredients.label: Neutral oil (or light olive oil)
ingredients.notes: Helps the sauce emulsify and become glossy.

ingredients.label: Saltine crackers (or white bread), torn
ingredients.notes: For gluten-free, use GF crackers. Bread option: ~1/2 cup soft white bread.

ingredients.label: Lime juice
ingredients.notes: Optional but brightens; adjust based on cheese tang.

ingredients.label: Black olives
ingredients.notes: Peruvian botija olives are traditional; use any good black olives.

steps.title: Boil the potatoes
instructions: Place potatoes in a pot and cover with cold water by at least 1 inch.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer until potatoes are tender when pierced.
Drain, cool slightly, then peel (optional) and slice 1/2-inch thick.
checks: Knife slides into potato with slight resistance (not falling apart).

steps.title: Blend the sauce base
instructions: Add ají amarillo paste, queso fresco, crackers (or bread), and evaporated milk to a blender.
Add optional onion and garlic if using.
Blend until very smooth; scrape down sides as needed.
checks: Sauce is smooth with no cracker chunks.

steps.title: Emulsify, season, and adjust thickness
instructions: With blender running on low, drizzle in oil to emulsify and make the sauce glossy.
Taste and season with remaining salt (start with 1/2 tsp, then adjust).
Add optional lime juice and black pepper to balance richness.
If too thick, add 1–2 tbsp milk/water at a time. If too thin, blend in 1–2 more crackers.
checks: Sauce coats the back of a spoon (nappe).
Flavor is creamy, slightly spicy, and balanced (not overly salty).

steps.title: Plate and garnish
instructions: Arrange lettuce leaves on a plate (optional).
Spoon huancaína sauce generously over potatoes.
Garnish with hard-boiled egg halves and black olives if desired.
Serve cool or at room temperature.
checks: Sauce is thick enough to cling to potatoes; plate looks clean and bright.

menuIdeas: Starter before lomo saltado or pollo a la brasa
Side for grilled meats and simple rice

refrigerate.notes: Store sauce separately if possible for best texture; stir before serving.

notes: Sauce can be made 24 hours ahead; it thickens in the fridge. Loosen with a splash of milk before serving.
Potatoes can be boiled ahead; store peeled/sliced covered in the fridge.

weeknightTips: Use pre-cooked potatoes and eggs for a fast plate.
Make sauce once and use it as a dip/spread for sandwiches or roasted vegetables.
scalingNotes: Sauce scales linearly; blender needs enough volume—use a smaller jar for half batches.

notes: Ají amarillo varies by brand; some pastes are very salty or very spicy. Adjust salt/heat accordingly.
If your sauce tastes bitter, the ají paste may be strong—add more cheese/milk and a touch of lime to balance.
For a more traditional flavor, add a small piece of fresh ají amarillo (seeded) if available and blend well.
Use 1–2 tbsp ají amarillo paste and increase cheese to 10 oz.
Add 1–2 tsp lime juice to keep it bright without heat.
Use 1/2 cup soft white bread instead of crackers, OR blend in 1/2 of a cooked potato slice to thicken.
Use gluten-free crackers as the thickener, or thicken with a small portion of cooked potato.
Verify ají paste has no gluten-containing additives.
Make sauce slightly thicker by adding 2–3 extra crackers.
Serve with crudités, chips, roasted potatoes, or as a sandwich spread.
Keep sauce refrigerated (dairy). Do not leave at room temperature for extended periods.
Use clean utensils to avoid cross-contamination when serving buffet-style.
